smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: something for H2Ocean
thread.body_text:
hi Edward, just went through your site, not sure how H2Ocean isn't already on my client list haha.

that's not really why I'm here though. I do Meta ads for ecom brands, and rather than pitch you some BS, I just went ahead and built you a few.

built three ad mockups from H2Ocean's actual products, plus a word-class brand playbook I put together from your site and reviews. it's finished and ready to go on my end.

want me to send it your way?

- Kyle

Kyle 

 Sure send me what you are presenting for Meta Ads, we are interested in the creative content.
 

 Thanks
 Eddie

here you go Edward - 2 ad mockups built from H2Ocean's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

thread.subject: Re: something for H2Ocean
thread.body_text:
Just set us up for tomorrow. 

 Thanks
 Eddie

copy.tldr: H2Ocean is a 20-year-old natural sea salt mouthwash brand with real products and zero Meta ads running, so this call is about starting clean from their bestselling Arctic Ocean Mint, not fixing a broken account.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A natural oral care brand out of Stuart, Florida that has been around for over 20 years. They make the original patented sea salt mouthwash. They sell direct on their site and also talk to dental professionals.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Two products. Healing Rinse Mouthwash in Arctic Ocean Mint and in Fresh Lemon Ice. Both use hand-harvested Red Sea salt, a natural enzyme, and xylitol. No alcohol, no fluoride, no artificial stuff. Sold in 8oz, 16oz, and 64oz sizes. Note the site prices vary by page, so confirm real price on the call.
deep_dive.positioning: A premium, natural, non-burning alternative to harsh alcohol mouthwashes. Built around healing and recovery, not just fresh breath.
deep_dive.who_buys: Health-conscious adults recovering from dental work, oral piercings, canker sores, or sore throats. People who want something gentle they can use daily and give to the whole family.
customer_voice: This is great for post-dental work and for oral piercings! It was the only thing that helped my tongue piercing heal. Two thumbs up
strengths: Long track record, 20-plus years and a patented formula, which is rare and hard for competitors to copy.
Clear healing use cases. People with piercings, canker sores, and post-dental pain are actively looking for relief, and this product speaks straight to them.
gaps: No reviews visible anywhere on the site, so there is no social proof to lean on in ads.
No Meta ads found running, so there is no past data to build on. We are starting from zero.

game_plan.headline: Start with the healing angle on Arctic Ocean Mint, prove one ad works cheap, then widen and add Lemon Ice once it is back in stock.

phases.goal: Prove that paid ads can bring in buyers at a price that works. Find one ad and one audience that click.
campaigns: One sales campaign with 2 to 3 ad angles from the Scroll-Stopper Sheet
Broad targeting first, let Meta find the buyers, no fancy audience tricks yet
lead_products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Arctic Ocean Mint. This is the one to lead with. It is in stock, mint is the most familiar mouthwash flavor to a cold audience, and the Lemon Ice product page shows sold out, so we cannot send traffic to it. Confirm stock on the call.
phases.creative: Go live first with the healing and recovery ad from the sheet, the one that speaks to piercings, canker sores, and post-dental pain. That is the angle where this brand is clearly different and where people are in real discomfort and ready to buy. Run the alcohol-free and gentle angle as the second test.
phases.budget: Start small and steady so we can learn without burning money.
phases.what_success_looks_like: One ad clearly beating the others, and first sales coming in at a cost we would both be happy to repeat. Not scale yet.

phases.goal: Put more money behind the winning ad and audience. Start bringing back people who visited but did not buy.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad from month one with more budget
Add a simple retargeting campaign for site visitors and add-to-carts
lead_products: Still Arctic Ocean Mint as the main driver. If Lemon Ice is restocked, test it as a second flavor option for people who already know the brand.
phases.creative: Make 2 to 3 fresh versions of whatever won, so the ad does not get stale. Add customer quotes to the ads once we have permission to use real reviews.
phases.budget: Step budget up only on what is already working. No big jumps.
phases.what_success_looks_like: The winning ad still holding its cost as we spend more, plus early sales coming back from retargeting.

phases.goal: Scale what works and add the second product properly.
campaigns: Scale the main campaign wider to new cold audiences
Add a proper campaign for Fresh Lemon Ice as a second flavor, assuming it is back in stock
lead_products: Arctic Ocean Mint as the workhorse. Fresh Lemon Ice added as a second option and for repeat buyers who want variety.
phases.creative: Build ads around the specific use cases that pulled best, for example a piercing-healing ad and a post-dental ad, so each one talks to one type of buyer.
phases.budget: Higher, but only in step with results holding steady.
phases.what_success_looks_like: A steady, repeatable spend that brings back more than it costs, with two products selling instead of one.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: enough to get roughly 30 to 40 clicks a day at typical costs in this category, which is the floor for learning which ad and audience work before we spend more

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We cannot give an honest number yet
estimates.basis: the site shows two different prices for the mouthwash, $17 on one page and $11 on another, and we have no view of their repeat-buy rate or margin. Need the real average order value and margin from Eddie before promising a revenue figure. Using $11 as a stand-in AOV, this is a low price to carry paid ads on a single bottle, so the real math depends on bundles and repeat buyers.

assumptions: Arctic Ocean Mint is in stock and can be shipped at volume.
Fresh Lemon Ice can be restocked before month three, since its product page currently shows sold out.
The real selling price is higher than $11, or they sell larger sizes and multi-packs, because $11 alone is thin for paid ads.
There are real customer reviews somewhere, even if not on the site, that we can get permission to use as social proof.

questions.question: Are you running any paid ads right now, on Meta or anywhere else?
questions.listening_for: We found no Meta ads live. Confirm this. If they never advertised, we are teaching from scratch. If they stopped, ask why it stopped, that tells us a lot.

questions.question: Who handles marketing for you today, in-house or an agency?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is anyone to work with, and whether a past agency left a bad taste. Shapes how much hand-holding they expect.

questions.question: When someone buys, what do they usually spend, and do they come back and buy again?
questions.listening_for: The real average order value and repeat rate. This is the number that decides if $11 bottles can carry ads. Bundles and repeat buyers change everything.

questions.question: Roughly what does it cost you to make and ship a bottle, so we know what room we have to spend?
questions.listening_for: Their margin. At an $11 price, if margin is thin, we need bundles or bigger sizes to make ads work. Do not push spend if there is no room.

questions.question: I noticed there are no reviews on your site. Do you have reviews or testimonials somewhere we could use?
questions.listening_for: This is a big one. Ads need proof. If they have reviews on Amazon or elsewhere, great. If they have none at all, that changes the creative plan and we lead harder on the patent and the 20-year story.

questions.question: The Lemon Ice page shows sold out. Is that a stock issue, and how is stock overall?
questions.listening_for: If they cannot keep product in stock, scaling ads will make it worse. We plan around what they can actually ship.

questions.question: Which product and which bottle size sells the most for you?
questions.listening_for: Confirm Arctic Ocean Mint is the bestseller before we build around it. If a bigger size sells better, that helps the price math a lot.

questions.question: Of all the uses you list, piercings, canker sores, post-dental, which one brings you the most buyers?
questions.listening_for: The strongest use case to lead the first ad with. If piercings drive most sales, we lead there instead of general daily use.

questions.question: When someone lands on your site, do they usually buy, or drop off?
questions.listening_for: Whether the problem is getting traffic or converting it. If the site does not convert, no ad will save it and we fix that first.

questions.question: Are you showing up when people search things like sea salt mouthwash or mouthwash for oral piercings on Google?
questions.listening_for: People clearly search for these problems. Bad breath, canker sores, and piercing aftercare are real searches. This is an honest opening for Google search ads and SEO, since demand already exists. Note if they say they rank for nothing.

questions.question: Do you sell on Amazon too, or only your own site?
questions.listening_for: If reviews live on Amazon, we can borrow that proof. Also tells us where their real sales come from and whether Google should point to Amazon or their site.

objections.objection: We have never run ads and are not sure they work for us.
objections.answer: That is exactly why we start small. We put a low daily budget behind a few ads for the first month just to learn what works. We do not scale until something proves itself. You risk very little to find out.

objections.objection: Our mouthwash is only $11, ads will cost more than we make.
objections.answer: Fair point, and that is why the first thing I want to understand is your repeat buyers and your bigger sizes. If people come back or buy the 16oz and 64oz, the math changes. We can also test simple bundles. I will not push spend if the numbers do not support it.

objections.objection: We are just interested in the creative, not a full service.
objections.answer: Totally fine. The ads and playbook are yours either way. On the call I will just walk you through how I would actually run them, so even if you take it in-house you know the plan. No pressure.

objections.objection: We do not have reviews to put in ads.
objections.answer: Understood. That is why we lead on your real strengths first, the patent and the 20-year track record, which most competitors cannot claim. Meanwhile we can start collecting reviews from real buyers so the ads get stronger over time.

red_flags.flag: No reviews anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Ads live and die on proof. With no visible social proof, the first ads have to lean on the patent and the brand story, and results may be slower until we gather real reviews.

red_flags.flag: Price is low at $11, and two pages show two different prices ($11 and $17).
red_flags.why_it_matters: A low single-bottle price is thin for paid ads. The price mismatch also suggests the site needs a look. We must confirm real price, sizes, and margin before promising revenue.

red_flags.flag: Fresh Lemon Ice shows sold out.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We cannot send paid traffic to a product people cannot buy. It also hints at possible stock problems, which would hurt any attempt to scale.

red_flags.flag: Health and healing claims are tightly limited.
red_flags.why_it_matters: The playbook bans cure, fixes, and cancer claims. Meta is strict on health products. Copy must stay careful or ads get rejected, which slows everything down.

other_channel_openings: Google search ads. People actively search for things like sea salt mouthwash, mouthwash for oral piercings, and canker sore relief. Real demand already exists, so search is an honest fit.
SEO through their Knowledge Center. They already have articles and clinical studies pages. That content can be built up to rank for the same healing searches over time.
